coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with full-color-tunable room-temperature phosphorescence for photo-stimulated responsive application
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wenhai
Chang, Qing
Li, Ling
Li, Jian-An
Yue, Dewu
Su, Shichen
coredata.dc:description: Advances in polymeric matrices with ultralong and color-tunable phosphorescence were recently made, but their luminescence mechanism was not yet thoroughly understood. The current experimental and theoretical studies show that the red-shift of phosphorescence spectrum is caused by increasing the C–N and C=O of carbon dots in the polymeric matrix . In addition, theoretical calculations explain the charge transfer and spin orbit coupling of carbon dots in a system containing different covalent bonds. The phosphorescence emission color and lifetime can be modulated, which is applied into triple anti-counterfeiting of photo-stimulated-dependent color, time-dependent color, and time-dependent phosphorescence lifetime. In the application, the phosphorescence color of carbon dots in the polymeric matrix can be varied from deep blue (431 nm) to red (620 nm) with a maximal lifetime of 2.02 s and a maximum phosphorescence quantum yield of 20.1%. This work provides a new direction for the development of carbon dots with efficient and color-tunable ultra-long phosphorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Full-color emission
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Triple-mode anti-counterfeiting
Polymeric matrices
Photo-stimulated responsive materials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The mechanism of color-tunable afterglow materials

U22A2073 ); Rural Science and Technology Commissioner Project (Grant No. KTP20210340 ); The Scientific and Technological Plan of Guangdong Province, China (No. 2022A0505050067 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Solid-phase synthesis of polymeric matrix. (a) The matrix is synthesized by urea as the phosphorescence core, while o-phenylenediamines and p-phenylenediamines are used as doping agents to regulate the phosphorescence color. The names of the color-tunable polymeric matrix by incorporating diverse phenylenediamines. TEM (up) and SEM (down) images of (b) CDs@UM, (c) CDs@UO, (d) CDs@UP, (e) CDs@UOM, and (f) CDs@UOP.

XRD, FT-IR, and XPS characterization of polymeric matrix of CDs@UM, CDs@UO, CDs@UP, CDs@UOM, and CDs@UOP. (a) XRD patterns. (b) FT-IR spectra. (c) Raman spectra. The XPS spectra and fitting results of (d) C1s, (e) N 1s and (f) O1s.

The color-tunable carbon dots in polymeric matrix from CDs@UM, to CDs@UOP. The corresponding Phos. emission under (a) 365 nm and (b) 450 nm. (c) Fluorescence and phosphorescence QYs under the excitation of 365 nm. (d) Phosphorescence decay time under excitation of 330 nm. (e) The phosphorescent photographs under radiation 365 nm (UV), and white light off.

Red-shift afterglow mechanism of polymeric matrix. Diagrams of calculated energy levels for CDs@UO (a, d), CDs@UP (b, e) and CDs@UOP (c, f). H, L and f represent the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the oscillator strength for CDs@UO (g), CDs@UP (h) and CDs@UOP (i), respectively.

Triple anti-counterfeiting of polymeric matrix. (a) “SCNU” pattern under vary radiations. The pattern of “S”/“N” and “C”/“U” are filled with the powders of CDs@UOM and CDs@UOP, respectively. (b) Photographs of “SCNU” under 302 nm, 365 nm, and white light on and off. (c) The first, second and third rows correspond to the Phos. emission, under 302 nm, 365 nm, and white light radiations, respectively. (d) Photographs of “SCNU” under 302 nm lighted off during time from 1 to 7 s. (e) Phos. lifetime of CDs@UOM and CDs@UOP under 330 nm radiation on behalf of timed-dependent lifetime. (f) Phos. emission of CDs@UOM from 0.016 (red) to 5.4 s (blue) for time-dependent color.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with full-color-tunable room-temperature phosphorescence for photo-stimulated responsive application

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Semiconductor Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Advances in polymeric matrices with ultralong and color-tunable phosphorescence were recently made, but their luminescence mechanism was not yet thoroughly understood. The current experimental and theoretical studies show that the red-shift of phosphorescence spectrum is caused by increasing the C–N and C=O of carbon dots in the polymeric matrix . In addition, theoretical calculations explain the charge transfer and spin orbit coupling of carbon dots in a system containing different covalent bonds. The phosphorescence emission color and lifetime can be modulated, which is applied into triple anti-counterfeiting of photo-stimulated-dependent color, time-dependent color, and time-dependent phosphorescence lifetime. In the application, the phosphorescence color of carbon dots in the polymeric matrix can be varied from deep blue (431 nm) to red (620 nm) with a maximal lifetime of 2.02 s and a maximum phosphorescence quantum yield of 20.1%. This work provides a new direction for the development of carbon dots with efficient and color-tunable ultra-long phosphorescence.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic phosphorescent materials are widely used in bioimaging, anti-counterfeiting, data encryption, and displays due to their advantages of low cost, low toxicity, and simplicity in synthesis [1–5]. Currently, organic materials with color-tunable phosphorescence have been developed via the strategies of aggregation state and clustering-triggered emission [6–13]. In most cases, their excitation-dependent phosphorescence originates from the formation of different aggregates or oxygen clusters in the system [11–13]. On the other hand, strategies including host-guest [14,15] and energy transfer [16,17] introduce feasibility of regulating the phosphorescent color through bringing in the guest phosphors or emitter dopants in the networks. Guest materials (as dopants) were employed to regulate the phosphorescent color, and the host materials interact with the guest to activate the phosphorescence emission [6,15,18–21]. However, the mechanism of long wavelength phosphorescence is still unclear.

Polymeric matrix based on urea has attracted great attention due to its abundant surface functional groups (C=O), which make it a potential candidate to realize high performance phosphorescence [22]. Recently, Lin et al. embedded different types of carbon dots (CDs) as dopants in a urea system and synthesized the composite with tunable ultra-long phosphorescence and emission colors from blue to red [23]. Lin et al. reached the phosphorescent emission wavelength can be adjusted from 462 to 623 nm by changing the feeding ratio and reaction temperature [24]. The mechanism behind the formation of phosphorescent emission with CDs and their emission intensity dependency has been studied by the C=O moieties [25]. Besides, cross-linking enhances the photoluminescence quantum yield of carbonized polymer dots, in which confined-domain promotes the energy level overlap, redshifts emission wavelength, and facilitates phosphorescence generation [26–29]. Nevertheless, the exploration of phosphorescence mechanism was not yet fully understood due to the intricate components of the carbon dots [30]. Fluorescence materials based on phenylenediamine isomers (m-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, and p-phenylenediamine) with the different positions of the amino group have displayed the redshifted optical absorption and emission properties owing to the disparate content of the π-conjugated domains [31]. With a proper design, molecular engineering could provide a versatile platform with a color-tunable phosphorescence capability [32]. To obtain the ultra-long phosphorescence of organic materials, several strategies have been proposed, including the incorporation of substituent(s) with lone-pair electrons, introduction of heavy halogen atom(s), crystallization, polymerization, and host–guest doping [2,22,33–35]. Moreover, the dopants could improve the brightness and emission duration through efficient radiative decay and the trapping of electrons, respectively [1,16]. Organic phosphors with ultralong lifetimes (>1 s) remain very limited.

In this study, color-tunable ultra-long phosphorescence carbon dots in polymeric matrices were realized by incorporating phenylenediamine and urea into one system. The phosphorescence color of carbon dots in polymeric matrix can be modulated from deep blue (431 nm) to red (620 nm) with their lifetimes varied from 0.31 s to 2.02 s under the different radiation and the phosphorescent quantum yield (Φ P) from 4.4 to 20.1%. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) studies were performed, revealing that the hydrogen-bonded system consisted of the related functional groups of N–H, C=O, and C–OH in the polymeric matrices. The red-shift of phosphorescence (Phos.) was associated with the increasing conjugated content of carbon dots in the polymeric matrix, as evidenced by the results of XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy studies. Theoretical calculations explain the charge transfer and spin-orbit coupling of long-wave phosphorescence in the case of covalent bond between carbon dots itself and matrix urea. The phosphorescence color and lifetime can be modulated, which is applied in triple anti-counterfeiting of time-dependent color, photo-stimulated-dependent color, and time-dependent phosphorescence lifetime.
Urea (>99%) were purchased from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. m-phenylenediamine (>99.5%), o-phenylenediamine (>99.5%), and p-phenylenediamine (>97%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd.

The TEM images were obtained by JEM-2100HR. XPS measurements were performed using AXIS SUPRA spectrometer. The radicals of polmyeric urea were characterized by a Bruker VERTEX-70 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected by BRUKER D8 ADVANCE. The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were recorded by FLS980, respectively. Phosphorescence lifetimes and fluorescence quantum yield were characterized by using FLS980. Raman measurements were performed using inVia spectrometer.

Synthesis of CDs@Uet: CDs@Uet was synthesized by 0.6 g urea and 100 μl ethanol in a 25 mL Teflon for heating at 200 °C for 6 h. The autoclave was then allowed to cool down to room temperature naturally. The powder was then washed three times with ethanol and water (1:1) to remove impurities. Prepare the p-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, and m-phenylenediamine (0.4 mg/mL) dissolved in ethanol. Then, synthesis of CDs@UM, CDs@UO, CDs@UP, CDs@UOM, and CDs@UOP are the same as CDs@Uet, except for the addition of different amounts of p-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, and m-phenylenediamine: 0.6 g urea and extra 100 μl solution containing m-phenylenediamine (CDs@UM); 0.6 g urea and extra 100 μl solution containing o-phenylenediamine (CDs@UO); 0.6 g urea and extra 100 μl solution containing p-phenylenediamine (CDs@UP); 0.6 g urea and extra 100 μl solution containing o-phenylenediamine and 100 μl m-phenylenediamine (CDs@UOM); 0.6 g urea and extra 100 μl solution containing o-phenylenediamine and 100 μl p-phenylenediamine (CDs@UOP).

Samples (CDs@UOM, CDs@UM, CDs@UO, CDs@UP, and CDs@UOP) with different dopants (m-phenylenediamines, o-phenylenediamines, p-phenylenediamines) were synthesized with the color-tunable phosphorescence (Fig. 1 a) [35,36]. Detailed synthesis steps are included in the Supplementary Information. CDs@Uet was synthesized by urea under the same conditions for comparison (Figs. S1-S3). Studies of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were conducted and their results were shown in (Tables S1–S4). In Fig. 1b–f, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showed that the samples were blocky or nanosheet, indicating the formation of polymers. The TEM images of samples show nanoparticles embedded into the polymeric matrix, indicating that dopants may exist as polymer dots.

From the XRD results (Fig. 2 a), the diffraction peaks can be discerned characteristic of urea at 22.6° and 35.6 °for CDs@Uet [37]. The corresponding diffraction patterns of CDs@UM, CDs@UO, CDs@UP, CDs@UOM and CDs@UOP have peaks at 22.48°, 22.17°, 22.55°, 22.34°, and 22.29° are lower than that of Uet, suggesting doping alters the lattice structure (Fig. S1) [38,39]. This means that the gaps in the lattice become larger, possibly with the formation of carbon dots. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2b) show characteristic peaks at 785(N–C=N), 1154 (C–N), 1542 (C=C), 1720 (C=O), 2801 (C–H), 3213 (N–H), and 3457 cm−1 (-OH), respectively. As compared to Uet, the FT-IR spectra of CDs@UM, CDs@UO, CDs@UP, CDs@UOM, and CDs@UOP show gradually increased intensities in the characteristic peaks at 1154 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 respectively associated to the C–N stretching vibrations and the C=O stretching vibrations. This indicates the covalent crosslinked copolymerization of the related function groups. From FT-IR spectra, the stronger peaks for CDs@UM, CDs@UO, CDs@UP, CDs@UOM, and CDs@UOP show at around 785 cm−1 (N–C=N stretching vibrations) further reveal the dopants themselves and also the polymerization reaction. As shown in Fig. 2c, Raman spectra present three evident peaks of symmetric -C-N stretching, symmetric C=C stretching and symmetric C=O deformation at 1012 and 1542 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1, respectively [40–42], further demonstrating the different degrees of polymerization of the matrix. The high degree of graphitization of the CDs@UOM and CDs@UOP are revealed in the much stronger C=O stretching in the Raman spectra than those of CDs@UM, CDs@UO, and CDs@UP, indicating the higher polymerization of phenylenediamines in the matrix (x). As phenylenediamines and urea have the same C–NH2 group, the polymerization reaction not only occurs in phenylenediamine but also in phenylenediamine and urea according to the increased content of -C-N [30]. This speculation could be further confirmed by other features. The results from the XPS spectra in Fig. 2d–f further support the FT-IR data. The XPS spectra demonstrate that CDs@Uet, CDs@UM, CDs@UO, CDs@UP, CDs@UOM, and CDs@UOP mainly consist of C, N, and O elements (Table S1, S2, and Fig. S5a). The C 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2d) indicate the presence of sp2 C (284.6 eV), C–N (286 eV), and N–C=O (288.9 eV). The fitting spectra for N 1s (Fig. 2e) show three peaks at 398.3 and 399.5, and 400.6 eV, which can be assigned to N–C=N, (C)3–N, and N–H bonds, respectively. The O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2f) exhibit two binding energies at 531.2 and 532.3 eV, indicating the presence of C=O and C–OH, respectively. Obviously, the increased content of C–N and C=O also corresponds to the further polymerization in terms of the C1s and O1s XPS spectra from CDs@UO to CDs@UOP. Meanwhile, it coincided with the XPS and FT-IR data that the hydrogen-bonded system including related functional groups of N–H, C=O, and C–OH exhibits in the polymeric matrix [23,30,43,44]. It is believed that the polymers are mainly composed of urea, and carbon dots as the dopants successfully incorporated in the polymeric matrix.

The phosphorescence emission peaks are located at 463, 474, 481, 520, 528 nm for CDs@UOM, CDs@UM, CDs@UO, CDs@UP, and CDs@UOP, respectively under the 365 nm light (Fig. 3 a). Under the white light wavelength centered at 450 nm, the phosphorescence performance is investigated by the 450 nm excitation (Fig. S6). The phosphorescence emission peaks are located at 533, 533, 588, 593, 617 nm for CDs@UM, CDs@UO, CDs@UP, CDs@UOM, and CDs@UOP under the exaction of 450 nm, respectively (Fig. 3b). Notably, the samples synthesized using three precursors, such as CDs@UOM and CDs@UOP, have weak bimodal emission. The supporting image shows a sample of a single synthesis of two precursors, where the spectra of CDs@UOM and CDs@ UOP overlap with the spectra of CDs@ UM, CDs@UO and CDs@UP, speculating that CDs@UOM or CDs@UOP bimodal emission may contain CDs@UM, CDs@UO or CDs@UP (Fig S7). This means that it might have multiple photoluminescent sites [24,45]. The phosphorescence colors of carbon dots in polymeric matrix for CDs@UM, CDs@UO, CDs@UP, CDs@UOM, and CDs@UOP can be modulated from deep blue (431 nm) to red (620 nm) with absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 33.2, 29.6, 25.5, 24.2 and 9.8% while the phosphorescent quantum yield (PhQY) of 20.1, 16.5, 11.7 13.7 and 4.4% (Fig. 3c). CDs@UM, CDs@UO, CDs@UP, CDs@UOM, and CDs@UOP have phosphorescence lifetimes of 1.58, 1.17, 1.23, 2.02, and 1.01s, respectively (Fig. 3c and Table S3). Fig. 3d presents the phosphorescence of the as-prepared samples from the blue to red color range when the 365 nm light and white light just being switched off. Ultra-long phosphorescence was observed of CDs@UOM compared to CDs@Uet crystalline powder under ambient conditions aft er the removal of the 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) lamp (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of color-tunable afterglow materials
ce:section.ce:para: Taken together, it is evident that the effect of carbon dots is mainly discussed since covalent crosslinking play an effective role in the property of phosphorescence. As phenylenediamines and urea have the same C–NH2 group, covalent crosslinking not only occurs in carbon dots but also in carbon dots and matrix (urea) according to the increased content of C–N [30]. As previously reported, the synthesis of samples of m-phenylenediamine and urea at high temperatures affects photophysical properties through covalent bonding [26,46–48]. Further, TEM image shows that CDs are attached to the polymer, which means that they may also have covalent bond binding between CDs and the polymer. Here, the effect of the covalent bond on phosphorescence color is mainly considered, because diverse samples vary greatly before and after doping (Figs S8 and S9).

Theoretical calculation based on density functional theory (DFT) was carried out. Molecules with different degrees of conjugation were designed and shown in Fig. 4 a–c and Fig S10. The self-polycondensation of phenylenediamine may form macromolecules with planar conjugated structures, and the XRD phase shows that urea is the main polymer matrix. In addition, the molecular structure was further improved according to the previous theoretical calculation works [47]. Therefore, macromolecules with different conjugated nitrogen doping were selected and covalently bonded to urea to study the optical properties Fig. 4d–f. The results suggested that the phosphorescence have the wavelength at 541, 561 and 626 nm, which agrees well with the experimental measurement for CDs@UO, CDs@UP and CDs@UOP. The phosphorescence for CDs@UO and CDs@UP may originate from these molecules with different degrees conjugated nitrogen [49]. Further, the red areas marked in the molecule are the locations where covalent bonding takes place. The reason why the lowest single triplet energy level of the molecule is higher may be because of the low conjugation degree, which means that the ability to inhibit its vibration or rotation in space is poor [20]. Similarly, it can be seen that deamination of molecules under low conjugation results in a reduction in the level of the single triplet state (Fig. S11) The energy band relationship calculated in the supporting file is helpful to the understanding of the relationship between structural configuration and energy band. In addition, it was found that the phosphorescent redshift of the sample could be promoted to the emission wavelength of 620 nm when two urea molecules were connected at the edge covalently. In addition, the influence of optical behavior caused by the polymerization of carbon dots themselves, the covalent bonds between carbon dots and matrix urea also have a great influence. This effect can be equivalent to increasing C=O content and is consistent with the phosphorescence spectrum of CDs@UOP sample. In addition, SOC (spin-orbit coupling) from S1 to T1 shows 0.72, 0.20 and 0.1 cm−1 for three molecules. SOC decreases as covalent bonds are further combined, which means that the luminescence efficiency of triplet exciton becomes worse and worse, which is relevant to experiments. In Fig. 4g–i, it shows that 45.9%, 61.4% and 47.5% of this transition mode can be described as a transition from the electrons occupying orbital HOMO to imaginary orbital LOMO+3 for CDs@UO, CDs@UP and CDs@UOP. Therefore, the nature of transitions can be explained by analyzing the dominant mode of orbital transitions [49]. Moreover, CDs@UO displays weaker molecular space restriction in comparison with those in CDs@UP and CDs@UOP due to covalent bonding. For this reason, intra-molecular charge transfer of CDs@UP and CDs@UOP become stronger, leading to small ΔE
ST values up to 0.34 and 0.39 eV (Fig. 4g–i).

On account of the excellent phosphorescent performance (Fig. S12), the samples of CDs@UOM and CDs@UOP were employed advanced applications in anti-counterfeiting. The phosphorescent color is regulated by CDs@UOM and CDs@UOP which are based on CDs@UO samples further added with m-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine dopants. As shown in Fig. 5 a, a triple anti-counterfeiting system was designed based on polymeric matrix loaded into the pattern “SCNU” (South China Normal University). We simply designed an alphabetic security code by using CDs@UOM and CDs@UOP. When light on, the pattern of “SCNU” shows almost the same color. The results of photo-stimulated-dependent color anti-counterfeiting are shown in Fig. 5b and c. It displayed the sequence of color from deep blue to blue when radiation of 302 nm switched off; the color from blue to green when 365 nm light was off; and the color from green to red when the white light turned off. Fig. 5d and e shows the results of time-dependent phosphorescence lifetime anti-counterfeiting. Due to the different phosphorescence lifetime for CDs@UOM and CDs@UOP, the “c” and “u” gradually disappeared, when time goes from 1 to 7 s after stopping the excitation. Time-dependent color anti-counterfeiting is shown in Fig. 5d and f. Blueshift of Phosphorescence emission was observed from 0.016 to 5.4 s, when the Phosphorescence emission was monitored every 16 ms. Such a unique phosphorescent phenomenon with a wider range of colors from deep blue to red is a promising strategy in high-level anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, color-tunable ultra-long phosphorescence carbon dots in polymeric matrices were fabricated by incorporating phenylenediamine and urea into one system. The phosphorescence color of carbon dots in polymeric matrix can be modulated from deep blue (431 nm) to red (620 nm) with their lifetimes varied from 0.31 s to 2.02 s under the different radiation and the phosphorescent quantum yield (Φ P) from 4.4 to 20.1%. XPS and FTIR studies were performed, revealing that the hydrogen-bonded system includes the related functional groups of N–H, C=O, and C–OH in the polymeric matrices. The red-shift of phosphorescence was caused by the increasing C–N and C=O of carbon dots in the polymeric matrix, as was clearly proved by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectroscopy studies. theoretical calculations explain the charge transfer and spin orbit coupling of carbon dots in a system containing different covalent bonds. The phosphorescence color and lifetime can be modulated, which is applied in triple anti-counterfeiting of time-dependent color, photo-stimulated-dependent color, and time-dependent phosphorescence lifetime.

Author Wenhai Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal Analysis, Writing - Original Draft; Author Qing Chang: Visualization, Investigation, Resources, Supervision Writing - Review & Editing; Author Ling Li: Writing - Review & Editing; Author Jian-An Li: Data Curation, Writing - Original Draft, Software, Validation; Author Dewu Yue, Shichen Su: Conceptualization, Funding Acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11974122. U22A2073); Rural Science and Technology Commissioner Project (Grant No. KTP20210340); The Scientific and Technological Plan of Guangdong Province, China (No.2022A0505050067).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2023.120017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organic phosphors with bright triplet excitons for efficient X-ray-excited luminescence

Xie, Y. Yang, J. Wang, Q. Chen, Y. Wang, H. Yang, G. Zhang, Z. An, X. Liu, W. Huang, Organic phosphors with bright triplet excitons for efficient X-ray-excited luminescence, Nat. Photonics 15 (2021) 187-192.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared light-emitting diodes utilizing a europium-activated calcium oxide phosphor with external quantum efficiency of up to 54.7

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Promoting single channel photon emission in copper(I) halide clusters for X‐ray detection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of highly efficient deep-blue organic afterglow through guest sensitization and matrices rigidification

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strongly enhanced long-lived persistent room temperature phosphorescence based on the formation of metal-organic hybrids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yang, D. Yan, Strongly enhanced long-lived persistent room temperature phosphorescence based on the formation of metal-organic hybrids, Adv. Opt. Mater. 4 (2016) 897-905.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen-bonded two-component ionic crystals showing enhanced long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence via TADF-assisted förster resonance energy transfer
